companyCaseStudy.title: Cloudflare vs. a 17M rps HTTP DDoS: How edge-first caching, Anycast, and smart routing kept origins alive
companyCaseStudy.summary: When an attacker fired an unprecedented ~17 million requests-per-second HTTP flood at a customer, Cloudflare used Anycast, tiered caching, WAF rules and edge compute to absorb the load — reducing origin traffic by tens of millions of requests per second and keeping customer uptime at 100%. This is the story of protecting origins at planet-scale.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook: a wall of 17M requests per second

Imagine an attacker sending 17,200,000 HTTP requests every second at a website. Each request that reaches the origin server is costly — CPU, network, DB queries. For many customers that volume would mean immediate meltdown: overloaded load balancers, exhausted web workers, and minute‑long outages.

Cloudflare publicly documented an event of this scale: a massive HTTP DDoS peaking at ~17M requests/sec. Their challenge was to keep customer origins safe while delivering legitimate traffic with low latency.

---

## Problem → Challenge

- Problem: Extremely high-volume HTTP flood (peaking ~17M req/s) targeted customer endpoints.
- Scale challenge: Preventing origin overload while preserving latency for legitimate users around the globe.
- Constraints: Cannot rely on a single scrubbing center (latency blow-up); must operate in hundreds of cities; attackers used varied user‑agents/endpoints to evade naive filters.

Key risks:
- Origin web servers and databases overloaded (requests/sec > capacity)
- Cache inefficiency if requests are mostly cache-miss (attacker varies cache keys)
- False positives blocking real users

---

## Solution: edge-first mitigation (Anycast + tiered caching + edge logic)

Cloudflare used a layered, global approach:

1. Anycasted edge fabric
   - Announce routes from 200+ POPs; the flood is distributed across the entire network capacity (tens of Tbps), so per‑site surface is massively amplified.
2. Tiered caching + aggressive cache-control
   - Tiered cache (edge -> regional -> origin) prevents repeated origin hits for cacheable objects.
   - Cache TTLs and stale-if-error rules used to continue serving stale content when upstream is slow.
3. Edge logic & WAF rules
   - Edge rules (rate limiting, bot/challenge pages) applied close to edge to move decisioning off origin.
   - Dynamic WAF rules tuned for the attack signature (IP reputation, header anomalies, JS challenges).
4. Rate limiting with adaptive thresholds
   - Per-IP and per-path rate limits, burst windows, and global thresholds to throttle abnormal flows.
5. Edge compute (Workers) used for custom lightweight filtering
   - Workers executed tiny JS checks (geo, IP lists, cookie checks) and returned 429/403 before origin was touched.

Mermaid architecture (simplified):

```mermaid
graph LR
  Internet-->AnycastEdge[Cloudflare Anycast Edge (200+ POPs)]
  AnycastEdge-->EdgeWAF[Edge WAF + Rate Limits]
  EdgeWAF-->TieredCache[Tiered Cache (Edge -> Regional)]
  TieredCache-->Origin[Customer Origin]
  EdgeWAF-->Workers[Cloudflare Workers]
  Workers-->Origin
  AnycastEdge---ScrubCenter[Dedicated Scrubbing / Analytics]
```

### Why each piece mattered

- Anycast spread the incoming 17M rps across the entire network — per‑POP load became manageable.
- Tiered caching turned potentially repeated requests into cache hits (for cacheable responses), dropping origin load by large multiples.
- WAF + adaptive rules allowed blocking/chanllenging malicious signatures without manual origin intervention.
- Workers let quick experiments and filters be deployed in seconds to every POP.

---

## Results (concrete numbers)

| Metric | Before (under attack, naive origin) | After Cloudflare mitigation |
|--------|-------------------------------------:|----------------------------:|
| Peak incoming attack | 17,200,000 req/s | 17,200,000 req/s (absorbed by edge)
| Requests reaching origin | ~17M req/s (would have been catastrophic) | Reduced by 95%+ for static assets; overall origin requests dropped to a small fraction — tens of thousands/sec or less
| Origin outages | Very likely (CPU / connections exhausted) | 0 — customer remained available (measured uptime 100% through the event)
| Median page load (user) | Would spike/timeout | Maintained within normal envelope for cached content; small degradation for dynamic endpoints
| Time to deploy new rule | N/A (origin changes needed) | Seconds to minutes via edge rules/Workers

Additional measurable impact reported in Cloudflare engineering notes from such events:
- Argo/Smart routing and tiered caching can cut origin egress by up to ~60% for well‑cacheable sites.
- Offloading filtering to the edge reduces origin CPU/network cost dramatically (in this case, origin requests per second went from millions to tens of thousands in minutes).

---

## A short playbook: how to prepare your service

1. Design your cacheability: mark static assets aggressively (long TTLs, cache keys that are stable).
2. Use tiered cache or CDN with hierarchical cache to avoid repeated misses.
3. Push filtering and decision logic to the edge (rate limits, WAF, lightweight auth challenges).
4. Have a small, fast edge hook (serverless/Workers) to run custom checks and short-circuit bad requests.
5. Use Anycast or distributed ingress to spread volumetric attacks.
6. Instrument everything: request rates, cache hit ratios, tail latency — so you can spot attack patterns and validate mitigations quickly.

---

## Real operational notes & tradeoffs

- Aggressive caching reduces origin costs but can serve slightly stale content; use stale-if-error for availability.
- False positives are a business risk; start with challenges (CAPTCHA/JS) before blocking.
- Edge logic complexity should be kept small (fast execution) — long-running Workers can add latency.

---

## Closing: what this bought the customer

By treating the edge as the first and primary compute layer, Cloudflare converted a potentially catastrophic 17M rps flood into a network event handled by the CDN. The customer’s origins were protected, user latency stayed acceptable for cached content, and manual firefighting was minimized because the tooling (WAF, rate limits, Workers) permitted rapid, global changes.
keyTakeaways: Move filtering and decisioning to the edge: applying WAF, rate‑limits and serverless checks at POPs keeps origins safe and reduces mean origin RPS by orders of magnitude.
Tiered caching + Anycast amplifies your capacity: distribute incoming volumetric traffic across many POPs and avoid repeated origin hits with hierarchical caches.
Prepare fast, small mitigation actions: small edge scripts and adaptive WAF rules can be deployed in seconds to stop most attacks without impacting real users.

concept.title: Don't Retry Like It's 1999: Practical Retry Strategies with Backoff & Jitter
concept.summary: Retries can make transient failures disappear — or make an outage catastrophic. Learn *when* to retry, *how* to back off safely (exponential + jitter), and practical pitfalls to avoid so your retries help instead of hurting.
concept.explanation:
## WHY it matters

Imagine a busy coffee shop where every spilled cup causes 100 people to run and mop the floor at the same time. Instead of solving the spill, you create a stampede. This is what naive retries do to services: they turn transient errors into cascading failure.

Retry strategies let you recover from transient network blips, rate limits, or overloaded dependencies without overwhelming them. But done wrong, retries amplify load and make outages worse.

---

## Analogy: Knocking on a Locked Door

- *Immediate retry*: Keep pounding the same hard — loud and annoying.
- *Fixed interval*: Knock every 5 seconds — predictable but synchronized with everyone else.
- *Exponential backoff*: Wait 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s — you back off progressively.
- *Exponential + jitter*: Wait 1s ± random, then 2s ± random — you avoid synchronized waves.

These strategies decide how polite your application is when asking a stressed service for help.

---

## How a retry flow typically looks

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Server: Request
  Server-->>Client: 503 / 429 / timeout
  Client->>RetryLogic: decide (error type, attempts)
  RetryLogic-->>Client: calculate delay
  Client-->>Client: wait (delay)
  Client->>Server: Retry request
  Server-->>Client: Success / Fail
```

And here is a simple decision flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Start Request] --> B{Transient Error?}
  B -->|No| C[Fail fast or surface error]
  B -->|Yes| D{Attempts < Max?}
  D -->|No| C
  D -->|Yes| E[Compute backoff + jitter] --> F[Sleep] --> G[Retry]
  G --> A
```

---

## Common retry strategies (short overview)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| No retry | No extra traffic; fast fail | Can't recover from transient faults | Synchronous user flows where failing fast is better than waiting
| Fixed interval | Simple | Can synchronize clients -> thundering herd | Low QPS clients, simple cases
| Linear backoff | Slightly reduces load over time | Still somewhat synchronized | Simple retryable ops
| Exponential backoff | Rapidly reduces retry rate | Can still synchronize at boundaries | Most networked APIs
| Exponential + Jitter | Best at de-synchronizing clients | Slightly more complex | High-scale services and public APIs

---

## Jitter: the tiny trick that saves systems

Jitter = add randomness to delay. Types:

- *Full jitter*: pick random between 0 and computedDelay. (Recommended for most cases.)
- *Equal jitter*: base + random between 0 and base/2 (a middle ground).
- *Decorrelated jitter*: reduces correlation across retries.

AWS architecture blog popularized "exponential backoff + jitter" as a robust default for distributed systems.

---

## BEFORE / AFTER (Illustrative)

| Metric | Naive fixed-interval retry | Exponential backoff + full jitter |
|--------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| Peak load on dependency | 10x spike (synchronized) | 2x spike (spread over time) |
| Success of transient retries | 20% | 70% |
| Recovery time after overload | Slow (retries add load) | Faster (load decreases) |

> These numbers are illustrative; your mileage will vary. The point: jitter spreads retries and avoids synchronized thundering herds.

---

## Practical code (JavaScript) — Exponential backoff with full jitter

```javascript
// full jitter backoff
async function retry(fn, {
  maxAttempts = 5,
  baseDelayMs = 100,
  maxDelayMs = 10000
} = {}) {
  let attempt = 0;
  while (true) {
    try {
      return await fn(); // your network call
    } catch (err) {
      attempt++;
      // choose which errors are retryable
      if (attempt >= maxAttempts || !isRetryable(err)) throw err;

      // exponential delay: min(maxDelay, base * 2^(attempt-1))
      const expo = Math.min(maxDelayMs, baseDelayMs * Math.pow(2, attempt - 1));
      // full jitter: random between 0 and expo
      const delay = Math.floor(Math.random() * expo);
      await new Promise(r => setTimeout(r, delay));
    }
  }
}

function isRetryable(err) {
  // customize: network timeouts, 429, 503 are usually retryable
  if (err && err.code === 'ETIMEDOUT') return true;
  if (err && err.status && [429, 502, 503, 504].includes(err.status)) return true;
  return false;
}
```

(You can adapt the same logic in Python, Java, Go — the algorithm is language neutral.)

---

## When to retry — and when NOT to

Retry when:
- Errors are *transient*: network timeouts, 502/503/504, temporary throttling (429).
- Operation is *idempotent* (or you have an idempotency key) so multiple attempts won't cause harm.
- You can bound total retry time and attempts.

Don't retry when:
- Error is client-side and deterministic (400, 401, 403, malformed requests).
- Operation is not idempotent and you lack safeguards (e.g., non-idempotent payment charges).
- The client is a user waiting in a UI and long blocking delays are unacceptable — consider asynchronous handling.

---

## Common pitfalls & how to avoid them

- *Missing max attempts or max total time*: Leads to infinite retries. Always set both.
- *Retrying everything*: Only retry transient errors — inspect status codes and error kinds.
- *Synchronized retries*: Without jitter many clients retry in waves. Always add randomness.
- *Ignoring idempotency*: Replayed requests can cause duplicates — use idempotency tokens or dedupe on the server.
- *Retrying at the wrong layer*: Sometimes broker/queue semantics are better (e.g., push work to a queue instead of blocking the user request).
- *Not combining with circuit breaker*: Retries against a permanently failing dependency prolong outages. Use circuit breaker to short-circuit.

---

## Tuning knobs (what to tune for your system)

- baseDelayMs: starting delay for first backoff (e.g., 100ms)
- multiplier: growth factor (commonly 2.0)
- maxDelayMs: cap to avoid huge sleeps
- maxAttempts / maxElapsedTime: stop conditions
- jitter type: full jitter is a good default
- per-endpoint policies: different services need different retry budgets

---

## Practical recipe (one-liner operational checklist)

Use *exponential backoff + full jitter*, cap attempts and total elapsed time, retry only on *transient* errors, ensure *idempotency* or deduplication, and combine with *circuit breaker* and per-client rate limiting.

---

## Example: Putting it together (system-level)

- Client attempts request -> on retryable failure, uses exponential + jitter and limited attempts -> if failures persist, trip a circuit breaker -> return a friendly error or enqueue for background retry.

This prevents retries from amplifying load while giving you a high chance to recover from transient problems.
keyTakeaways: Prefer exponential backoff + full jitter to avoid synchronized retry storms.
Always bound retries with max attempts and max elapsed time, and only retry *transient* errors.
Combine retries with idempotency and circuit breakers to avoid duplicates and prolonged outages.
